title: Binary Tree Level Order Traversal
description: Given the root of a binary tree, return the level order traversal of its nodes' values. (i.e., from left to right, level by level).
hints: Use a queue to perform BFS traversal.
Process nodes level by level by tracking the queue size at each level.
For each level, collect all node values and add them as a sublist to the result.

title: Binary Tree Level Order Traversal II
description: Given the root of a binary tree, return the bottom-up level order traversal of its nodes' values. (i.e., from left to right, level by level from leaf to root).
hints: Perform a standard BFS level order traversal.
Insert each level's result at the beginning of the result list to achieve bottom-up order.
Alternatively, reverse the final result array after standard BFS.

title: Binary Tree Right Side View
description: Given the root of a binary tree, imagine yourself standing on the right side of it, return the values of the nodes you can see ordered from top to bottom.
hints: BFS level order traversal is natural for this problem.
For each level, the last node visited is the one visible from the right side.
Keep track of the level size and capture only the last node at each level.

title: Binary Tree Zigzag Level Order Traversal
description: Given the root of a binary tree, return the zigzag level order traversal of its nodes' values. (i.e., from left to right, then right to left for the next level and alternate between).
hints: Use standard BFS level order traversal.
Track the current level number to decide the traversal direction.
Use a flag or level index to reverse every other level.

title: Minimum Depth of Binary Tree
description: Given a binary tree, find its minimum depth. The minimum depth is the number of nodes along the shortest path from the root node down to the nearest leaf node.
hints: BFS naturally finds the shortest path in an unweighted tree.
The first leaf node encountered during BFS gives the minimum depth.
Return the depth as soon as a node with no children is found.

title: Average of Levels in Binary Tree
description: Given the root of a binary tree, return the average value of the nodes on each level in the form of an array.
hints: Use BFS to traverse the tree level by level.
For each level, sum all node values and divide by the number of nodes.
Use floating point division for the average calculation.

title: Find Largest Value in Each Tree Row
description: Given the root of a binary tree, return an array of the largest value in each row of the tree (0-indexed).
hints: Use BFS to traverse the tree level by level.
For each level, track the maximum value seen.
Initialize the maximum to the minimum integer value before processing each level.

title: Populating Next Right Pointers in Each Node
description: You are given a perfect binary tree where all leaves are on the same level, and every parent has two children. The binary tree has the following definition: struct Node { int val; Node *left; Node *right; Node *next; } Populate each next pointer to point to its next right node. If there is no next right node, the next pointer should be set to NULL.
hints: BFS level order traversal naturally processes nodes left to right at each level.
While processing a level, connect each node to the next node in the queue.
Make sure to set the last node's next pointer to NULL.

title: 01 Matrix
description: Given an m x n binary matrix mat, return the distance of the nearest 0 for each cell. The distance between two adjacent cells is 1.
hints: Start BFS from all 0 cells simultaneously (multi-source BFS).
Initialize the distance for all 0 cells to 0 and enqueue them first.
For each cell popped from the queue, update its unvisited neighbors with distance + 1.

title: Rotting Oranges
description: You are given an m x n grid where each cell can have one of three values: 0 representing an empty cell, 1 representing a fresh orange, or 2 representing a rotten orange. Every minute, any fresh orange that is 4-directionally adjacent to a rotten orange becomes rotten. Return the minimum number of minutes that must elapse until no cell has a fresh orange. If this is impossible, return -1.
hints: Start BFS from all initially rotten oranges simultaneously (multi-source BFS).
Track the number of fresh oranges remaining. If it reaches 0, return the minutes elapsed.
If after BFS there are still fresh oranges remaining, return -1.
